ChimeraMapR  ·  v0.7.0

Using the ChimeraMapR Shiny app

A click-through walkthrough of the interactive app — upload three files, run the analysis pipeline stage by stage, and read the peak, LOH, fusion, and recombination-event tabs it produces, using real yeast chromosome I data the whole way through.

R ≥ 4.1 shiny::runApp("app.R") S. cerevisiae chrI test data

What you need first

  • R ≥ 4.1, with shiny, data.table, ggplot2, pracma, Matrix, Rcpp, and igraph installed.
  • A C++ toolchain (Xcode CLT on macOS) — chimera_functions.R compiles an Rcpp HMM the first time it's sourced.
  • Three input files: a read-level SNP call CSV, a SNP definition file (CSV or VCF), and a .fai chromosome size index. See Input files in the README for the exact schema — this tutorial uses the ready-made copies in test_data/.
No install step needed to try this. If you've cloned the ChimeraMapR repo, test_data/chrI.csv.gz, test_data/chrI_SNPs.vcf.gz, and test_data/ref_genome.fasta.fai are already there — every screen below was produced by uploading those three files as-is.
01

Launch the app

From the repo root, start the app with shiny::runApp() and let it open your default browser:

terminal
$ R -e "shiny::runApp('app.R', launch.browser = TRUE)"

Loading required package: shiny
Listening on http://127.0.0.1:XXXX

On macOS/Linux, symlink chimera.sh onto your PATH (e.g. as /usr/local/bin/chimera) to launch the same thing with a bare chimera command from any directory. The sidebar on the left holds file inputs and analysis parameters; the tab strip on the right is where every result lands.

02

Upload the three input files

Under Data Files, use the three Browse… buttons to supply the read-level SNP calls, the SNP definition file, and the chromosome-size index. Each shows an Upload complete bar once accepted — the SNP file accepts CSV, VCF, or gzipped VCF, auto-detected from the extension. Give the run a Sample Name (it's used in exported filenames and plot titles); REF/ALT strain names are optional and only affect legend labels on the LOH bands.

200 MB upload ceiling. The app raises Shiny's default request-size limit, but very large read CSVs should still be gzipped first — the read-data input accepts .gz directly.
03

Set analysis parameters

Minimum Run Length and Minimum Peak Height sit directly in the sidebar since they're the two knobs worth tuning on a first pass. Everything else lives behind the collapsed Advanced Settings disclosure — quality cutoffs, the Whittaker smoothing penalty, and the fusion/chain-analysis thresholds described below.

ParameterDefaultWhat it does
Minimum Run Length2Consecutive same-allele calls required to count as a sustained run; raise it for noisier or lower-coverage data.
Minimum Peak Height10Minimum chimeric read count for a peak to be reported — roughly half the median read depth is a reasonable start.
Minimum MAPQ / Base Quality20 / 10Read and base-quality floors applied before any allele classification happens (Advanced Settings).
Whittaker Lambda (λ)1Smoothing penalty — lower tracks sharp peaks tightly, higher smooths over jitter (Advanced Settings).
Jaccard Threshold for Auto-Fusion0.20Minimum shared-read Jaccard index between adjacent peaks to auto-fuse them in step 07 (Advanced Settings).
Min Spanning Reads / Peak Padding3 / 200 bpChain-analysis knobs used by the event caller in step 08 (Advanced Settings).

Leave everything at its default for this walkthrough — the chrI test set is clean enough that the defaults resolve every peak on their own.

04

Run the analysis

Click Plot Haplotype Chimeras. The app classifies every base call, detects chimeric transitions, builds the LOH and coverage maps (the compiled HMM), and renders the genome-wide overview — tracked by a progress bar since the LOH/coverage HMM passes are the slowest step. When it finishes, a notification confirms completion and a new Run Peak Fusion button appears in the sidebar.

Every result tab — Peak Summary, Individual Peak Plots, LOH Regions, Curve Fits, Read Statistics — populates from this single click. Peak Fusion and Recombination Events stay empty until you run the two follow-on steps below.

05

Read the Peak Summary and Individual Peak Plots tabs

Peak Summary lists every detected peak with its position, boundaries, height, supporting read count, and haplotype classification (binary, compound_binary, gene_conversion, internal_crossover, or undefined) — the same run-pattern label the CLI's --peak-list mode writes out.

Individual Peak Plots renders one panel per peak, per chromosome tab: every chimeric read spanning that peak, coloured REF (blue) vs ALT (red), with a haplotype-region bar underneath showing the called classification.

06

Check LOH Regions

The LOH Regions tab shows what the beta-binomial EM + Viterbi HMM called directly from per-position allele balance — independent of peak detection. Each row is a REF-fixed or ALT-fixed tract with its mean allele balance and standard deviation, a quick sanity check on how clean the underlying calls are.

07

Run Peak Fusion

Click Run Peak Fusion. Adjacent peaks already classified as gene_conversion or internal_crossover are excluded outright — the sidebar's About tab calls this the "Peak-Class Fusion Guard." Everything else is scored by shared-read Jaccard similarity and haplotype signature, then colour-coded:

  • Green — automatic fusion (Jaccard ≥ threshold, clean crossover or gene-conversion signature).
  • Yellow — supervised; check the box and click Apply Checked Fusions to approve it manually.
  • Red — not fused; the peaks are independent events.
  • Grey — unresolvable; not enough spanning reads with sufficient SNPs in all three zones to score.

Approving a supervised pair and clicking Apply Checked Fusions re-runs fusion with that pair merged, rebuilding the Fused Peak Plots tab in place — you don't need to re-run the whole analysis.

08

Call recombination events

Click Run Event Caller. This runs the chain-based scanner from loh_chain_analysis.R: it walks each chromosome's LOH tracts and fused peaks together, matches them against known recombination motifs, and assigns each event a confidence level — high when read and LOH support agree unambiguously, review when they don't.

Anything the chain scanner can't fold into a scored event — an LOH tract or peak left over after matching — shows up in the Other Events table beneath, rather than silently disappearing. On this run, everything resolved cleanly and that table is empty.

09

Export your results

Every tab has its own download button next to its table or plot, so you only need to fetch what you actually inspected:

Download Plot (.png) / (.rds)Overview Plot tab — genome-wide figure, or the raw ggplot object to re-style later
Download Peak DataPeak Summary tab — every detected peak with haplotype label
Download Fused Peak Data / Download Pair AnalysisPeak Fusion tab — fused groups and the underlying Jaccard scoring
Download LOH Regions (.csv)LOH Regions tab — REF/ALT-fixed tract boundaries and allele balance
Download Event Table (.csv) / Download Chain Objects (.rds)Recombination Events tab — final calls, or the full intermediate chain objects
Download Chimeric Read IDsRead Statistics tab — one read ID per line, for samtools view -N to pull a BAM of just the chimeric reads
Same pipeline, no UI. Once you know which parameters work for your data, chimera_cli.R runs the identical analysis from the terminal for batch processing — see the companion command-line tutorial.
Full parameter reference, input-file schemas, and the underlying analysis pipeline are documented in the repo README.md. This walkthrough used the chromosome I test set bundled at test_data/ — swap in your own bamCol.py-derived read CSV, VCF, and samtools faidx index to run it on real data.
